title: 2025/10/09 - PP
description: Thursday, 9 October 2025 | Primary Production Petition Committee of Ian Sizer: Madagascar Ragwort – A growing concern for Northland and the rest of New Zealand Bioeconomy Science Institute - AgResearch Dr Graeme Doole, Senior Scientist Dr Chris Buddenhagen, Senior Scientist Dr Nigel Bell, Senior Scientist Zachary Ngow, Senior Technician Ministry for Primary Industries Stuart Anderson, DDG Biosecurity New Zealand John Walsh, Director Pest Management
descriptionSummary: Primary Production. of Ian Sizer: Madagascar Ragwort – A growing concern for Northland and the rest of New Zealand. Bioeconomy Science Institute - AgResearch

chunks.text: If you could afford us a certain chunk of time and at 20 minutes at the end, that would be, uh, gratefully received. So I'll leave it with you and you can lead us off, Graham and whoever else wants to chip on that would be fantastic. Thanks. Uh, Koto and thank you chair for this invitation. Uh, we're really excited by the opportunity to talk to you as New Zealand's leading provider of weed research.

Uh, so the four of us are located within the AG Research group of the Bioeconomy Science Institute. Uh, I'm Graham Dol. I'm a group manager and a professor, um, with a background in weeds manage weed management for over 20 years. I introduced Nigel Bell, who's an expert in plant protection with 30 years experience. And Chris Buddenhagen is one of New Zealand's, uh, leading weeds researchers, especially in Biocontrol and Madagascar rag Ward.

I'd also like to, uh, introduce to you Zachary and Gail, who is, uh, the primary author of the Northland Regional Council Report that was done, uh, on Madagascar Rag earlier this year. And so we, we would like to take our submission as read and we invite questions on both that and any others that we may be qualified to answer.

Thank, thank you, Graham. I mean, please any other person online, by all means, chip, we've, we've got 20 minutes. If you've got any further remarks that you would like to offer. Uh, some of us has actually seen this stuff in the flesh, so we've got a fairly cognizant understanding of the difficulty that people face. Um, Nigel or Chris or Zachary, do you have anything to add to Professor Graham at this point?

Um, I'm not a biocontrol expert, but, um, just a weed expert, but yeah. Yes. Thank you. Uh, Nigel. I'm pleased. By all means, I'd, I'd, I'd really like to entertain some of your thoughts. If, if we can sort of have you, your vision of the world as it were.

Oh, okay. Yeah. Um, I won't add, uh, too much extra to what Grimes said other than, um, uh, oh yeah. I've been in agricultural research for, um, a long time now, and we are really, um, committed to helping out our farmers, um, and landowners. And so we are really keen to offer ourselves as to whatever, um, we can provide to help out in this, um, in this issue because we know it's pressing.

And, um, the landowner that are affected by it. Um, it is having a, a, you know, a large impact on them and would really like to help them out, but also provide any, um, help with the likes of containing the weed so that we get time to, um, come up with other solutions to help out other farmers before they become affected as well. Yeah, Yeah. Well, Nigel on, on that, if I may.

And, and Professor Graham, please, uh, cut me short if you need to. Um, we might as well launch into questions, 'cause there's a lot of them coming. I think the, the real issue for me as the chair of these wonderful people is, is that we're all really concerned about this as well and want to know where anecdotally it is in, in terms of spread in the island.

'cause I think that's the first port of call in terms of the response. If it's look gracious me from north to Taranaki, then we've got a, a much bigger problem. But if it's stuck in the Kaka Peninsula and, you know, around the re airport, then, uh, a notice of direction, having it notified as a national pest, all these sort of things can be tools at our disposal that warrant, uh, further, uh, ministerial oversight and, and, and coronial funding.

So, question one, where is it and where isn't it Chris? Most that isn't he? Yeah, Carry on. So, um, the evidence suggests, the strong evidence suggests that it's in Northland only, I think. But, um, as you know, it's a difficult to distinguish from another species, the gravel groundswell.

And that species is common all over, um, New Zealand, and especially in areas like Taranaki and, um, hawk space. So I would want to confirm any of those sightings by having photos and materials sent to us to identify. Uh, I'd say a key step would be to do more in this area.

Uh, okay. Chris, I've gotta cut can, and then I'm gonna go to Steve Abel. So if, if I volunteer, this has being a, a, a way forward that perhaps that you are, you are a suggesting that to do that you would require it to be considered a, a national pest and have it noted not as a notifiable weed rather than something that's localized.

So have it nationalized as it were, and then you would be seeking funding for the identification or the not therein. Is that my understanding? Yeah, we, we, we, you know, we're not regulators, we're scientists, so we support, um, by providing evidence for whomever needs it. And yes, um, the, you know, there's also these citizen science platforms like iNaturalist that have really good, um, AI based identification.

It may not be good enough to distinguish those two species. So it really needs a eye, uh, expert eye to be passed over those observations. So there's all kinds of tools to increase participation from that community of, um, people who use the iNaturalist platform, for example, and dedicated surveys to double check sites of concern would be a good, a, a good thing to, you know, double check if there's any, uh, suspected cases.

So, um, that could be a mix of biosecurity staff or scientists together. Yeah. Um, I'd just like to add that, yes, I agree about iNaturalist and, um, it's, it's something that I've been checking periodically just to see where it's spread. Um, but it seems to be picked up by some of the farmers in Northland.

Yes. And, and, um, just as part of an awareness campaign, it would be quite good to include that. Yes, It's, of course Zachary, I think, and naivety is probably our worst enemy, and apathy is also something that we have to grapple with. Mike Butter's Got a question. Ah, thank you chair. Um, I just had some questions around, um, some of the risk pathways.

And you talk about, um, you know, what particularly alarms me is hay and agricultural equipment or forestry equipment. And given that it was identified in 2022, and here we are three years later, and as a farmer, I sit there and look at the potential impact by either Pmu farm, uh, 65%, um, or 35% reduction in, uh, carcass weight per hectare.

That's pretty significant. And, um, I'm just interested in your thoughts into whether there could be a, um, a controlled area notice or something to try and restrict that or minimize that risk of spread, because I'm really concerned there would've been a lot of hay, potentially silage mm-hmm.

Uh, go out north and down into the white catto and things like that. So it feels like it, we need a bit more urgency. Just interested on your thoughts. I think in my, my personal opinion is, uh, and you know, I've done a, I've worked as an economic advisor to the government and, and, you know, evaluating different policy mechanisms, and it's very difficult to really answer what the best mechanism is, but we totally agree with the scientific, uh, justification for trying to restrict spread.

And so I think scientifically that makes a lot of sense, particularly because of the level of economic cost that exists. And that might, we don't know whether that 35% reduction is high or low, uh, given that we don't have much information on it, but it, we know that the spread it, it is, the spread is possibly quite significant and quite quick.

And so we totally, I, I think we have, we believe there's strong justification for limiting the spread. And just to sub on that too, would you also, um, think that waterways would be another, um, pathway for spread? I know certainly in mid Canterbury, um, irrigation ditches and nodding thistles are a great, uh, a great, uh, means of getting the spread seed spread everywhere.

Mm-hmm. Would you think that would be a risk up in north end as well? What would you say, Chris? Yeah, Yeah. Um, so we do, I think, I think so. But we should do some work on seed bank longevity. Um, the, in terms of the silage question, there's a lot of evidence from other weeds that silage almost always kills the seeds that are in silage.

We have some really tough weeds like, um, yellow bristle grass, for example, that dies in silage. So it's possible that silage isn't a risk, but hay would be, um, the, the, the timing of peak flowering and peak germination and, um, ex exactly when and where it'll, it's at the most productive stages is also important information.

And I do think that it would spread down ditches, of course, it would float along the water. And, and, you know, especially as water levels drop after a rain, you know, you could imagine that nice disturbed site being colonized. So of course, you know, that's a risk, I think, um, you know, more needs to be understood about when exactly it germinates the most.

So you, you could take strategies in, in a given area to reduce the probability of establishment in a, in a field at that time. Um, so, you know, there's a lot to be done. I just wanted to mention also that we'd done a weed hazard ranking for over 18,000, introduced plants in New Zealand, and this plant comes out on the top 10 for the north island, um, and in the top 300 for the south island, uh, Canterbury region.

So 300, the top 300 may not sound that high. It's certainly not as high as the north island, but that's out of 18,000 species that we, we assess. So that's in the top 5% still. So it's, it's, it, the other thing is that in Australia it's an annual, and in New Zealand, in Northland, it's a, it produces flowers year round.

So that's a distinction. It may, it may behave differently for the south in terms of the flowering period and so on. So, you know, there's a lot to be, um, sorted out in terms of understanding how the threat might play out just from basic biology, really simple science, but important science.

Thank you. Chris, I have a question for Miles Anderson. Yeah. Um, thanks for your time. Have you done any modeling on, on the spread as far as where you think how far will spread, um, next few years without anything being done and, and with various treatments?

Um, we, we do have some preliminary work, which we started out, um, with no funding. Me and another researcher from BSI, uh, formerly landcare. Um, and that model that we're running can actually test different intervention strategies and social interactions as well.

So it's a multidimensional model, and it can, can include climate change and climate suitability. So we'd really like to implement that model and, and test some of the different strategies. And it could be a way to test whether adaptive management is working. Um, so the indications are actually that it's been relatively slow.

It's probably been in Northland for a long time. You know, it was misidentified for years, so it's been relatively slow in Northland, but that doesn't mean that it's not gonna speed up. Um, so, um, we, we really don't know enough to say how far it's spread at this point. And I, I would say that's a key step. I wondered if Chris or Zach, whether you might be able to, uh, comment on the modeling that John Keen has done for the species and that we refer to in the submission.

Yeah, you wanna do that one, Zach? Yeah. Um, there were just a few parameters that, um, he wasn't quite sure about. So it is sort of a preliminary model And what could show Zach. Yeah, yeah. Uh, well, it did show that it is likely to be, um, suitable to a large amounts of the north island and parts of the south island, Large parts of the south island, actually. Yep.

I, I must confuse that, gentlemen. I've actually seen this stuff up in Car, car Peninsula a couple of times, um, being on site in the primary farm. So I am cognizant of it as a woody tissue plant that could look quite often likes beer, soil, and the way it goes. Mm-hmm. Um, kind of a question on the back of Mr. Anderson's question, um, but probably as much as a question as a statement, we've got our officials coming in from NPI after you lot.

Um, what's your wish list look like with this, really and truly now, I have heard that it would be having it as a nationally identified piece so we can wrap a campaign around it because, um, it's my understanding that the part of the war we're struggling with is misidentification, and that people are not cognizant of what they're wrapping, run, run, running a windrow through a baler and how it goes, et cetera, et cetera.

So, um, you know, you, you're on, you're on air, you're live, and we've got NPI officials in the room. What would you tell 'em? I think really in terms of the BSI mark, we, we really, we see that there's a very, there's a lot of uncertainty and we think that the issue needs more investment.

And I think really it comes down to prioritization. And I wondered if I pass to yourself, Chris, to speak about the areas that you might see as research priorities for this. We, um, yeah, so that national interest pest response, there's a number of other plants like Nigerian Wild, wild rice, um, for example, that have, um, kind of a national approach.

And I do think that a, um, it, it would be a shame to put all the responsibility on Northland region, um, alone. Um, especially if, you know, if we're holding the line really means trying to keep on top of those long distance dispersal events that really make you, um, kind of, that any long distance dispersal event that might occur by chance, say from human dispersal especially.

That's the kind of thing you want to get on top of immediately. And that's not necessarily just the leading edge in Northland, but anywhere else it could show up. So those, those kinds of responses do need a little bit of coordination, and it can't just be, um, one regional council or, um, there needs to be coordination too, in terms of revisiting sites.

If anything's been controlled, you really need to make sure you follow up and never forget to go back to any of the leading edge kind of infestations. Thank you. Chris, I've got a, sorry, I don't mean to interrupt you, Sue. I've got a question from Mike Butrick, and we are fairly thin on time, so, sorry. Thanks, Jim. I just had, I just had a question, probably two parts.

One is around chemical control and second is around, uh, potential, um, biological control in terms of chemical control. Are you aware, you know, currently people are using two four D and, uh, met sulfur type, uh, products, which actually have, um, some negative impacts on pasture and clovers. Are you aware of any other chemicals in other jurisdictions that, uh, could potentially, um, you know, be of benefit that we don't currently have available here?

Um, the chemicals used overseas are actually mostly harsher than the ones used in Northland, unfortunately. Yeah. Well, the other side is that, um, there's still some work to be done on surfactants and timing, and, you know, there, there could be some really, um, key and relatively straightforward research to be done that would improve the situation.

Even with the tools, we have different mixes and, you know, just formalizing a lot of these experiments that the farmers have already started, but doing it, you know, correctly could really, um, improve the situation and give them a few more tools and, and also understanding the perfect timing is often a key aspect of these things.

Yep. And in terms of, um, biological controls, um, I think I was reading through some of the stuff you, um, supplied to us previously. It was a little bit of work being done, I think it might've been in one of the African countries. Mm-hmm. Um, have you got any, um, research or in terms of what potentially could be biological controls?

Um, my impression from the feasibility study that came outta landcare is that there's one agent that's being tested for Australia, and they're, they haven't sorted out whether it'll attack any of the native CIOs in Australia or New Zealand. So that's, that would be one thing. They haven't done enough exploration to know that they've tracked all of the potential agents and Russ and fun fungi and that kind of thing haven't been investigated in full.

And then the agent that they released in Hawaii ended up attacking another non-native SIO and didn't lay eggs on the, um, the target plant and laid it more on this other sinesio or, or another species. So, um, it's like, you know, when they force it to lay eggs on, on that one, on, on the target, it does it, but when they're an alternative, it prefers the alternative.

So you, you know, there's a bit of, um, that's not a candidate necessarily, um, even though I wouldn't mind if that other, that other weed's also present here and is also a problem. But, you know, Thank you, gentlemen. I don't mean to be rude, but we've sort of met time. Um, this conversation's not going away and we'll pro probably, almost certainly get you back in, uh, for the same length of time or more as this, uh, problem con, you know, continues.

And we, we look for ways to alleviate, uh, you know, the pressure on rural communities and the damages about to do so. Thank you so much for your time, very grateful for your time, and have a wonderful afternoon. Thank You. Thank You for the invitation. No, thank you, James. Thanks a million. Thanks. Right, thanks a million. Exactly.

NPI officials are in the room. Stuart Anderson, always a pleasure to see you, sir. Yes, you see, you, um, you likewise, John. Um, please, by all means, grab a seat. We got your for 20, you are gonna get a fair degree of, uh, third degree about how we fix it, how we don't, what, what, what it look like, where we get money from, given that it's very little, um, and all of the above.

Obviously you heard the, uh, the hearing of evidence, then you lead off on what you think and yeah, let the team give you good some questions. Great doc. Uh, thank you. Um, and thanks for opportunity to come and talk to this. Um, uh, for those that don't know me, um, Stuart Anderson, deputy Director General by Security New Zealand, MPI, uh, and John Walsh, uh, who many of you will know is, uh, director of our peace management group, uh, in biosecurity at NPI as well.

Um, so look, just, just a few remarks and then, uh, yes, we can use the time, uh, to kick around some questions and discussion on what we do here. Um, like I say, um, appreciate the opportunity to come and talk about this. Uh, we do appreciate, uh, you know, the serious nature of this weed, uh, and the damage and the impacts that it, that it is having and can have, um, also acknowledge, you know, Mr.

Sizes petition and the points that he's made in it. Um, uh, his recommendations, uh, you know, largely, uh, you know, there's a sensible recommendations and we agree that, that those are the things that really should be, should be happening and should be focused on, um, particularly around, um, uh, more coordinated focus and drive on education, information, awareness and practices and things that can be done.

Mm-hmm. Um, you know, we not, and it's been referenced, um, late last year, uh, minister Hagar met with council, uh, et cetera up north. It was agreed out of that, that, um, this was best led and coordinated by councils and industry and stakeholder, um, partners in that, um, with us supporting, we are committed to supporting and doing what we can to help, um, that collaboration and coordination, um, and, uh, and have offered and continue to offer that support to the working group, uh, to drive some coordinated activity

and focus, uh, in this area, figure out what is realistic and what we can actually do about it across those areas of options. Um, having said that, totally acknowledge the role that MPI needs to play here as well, uh, and, uh, and help with, help with wherever we can to get traction and some action happening.

Um, and as I say, we are committed to, to playing our part in this, with that group, um, of councils and industry, uh, and whatever comes outta that in terms of feasible options on the table, um, control nationally coordinated communication, et cetera, um, to see what, see what we can, we can do together to deal with something that probably has been around for some period of time.

But, uh, but nonetheless, does, does pose a risk and does have a serious impact on people and farmers that are actually dealing with it at the moment. So, with that, um, I think if we, Can I lead off, and I'm gonna go straight to Mike, what would the counterfactual look like if this stuff did spread?

Okay. We're all cognizant that it's an issue as far as we're aware it's a localized issue. Uh, I think that's up for debate, and we haven't really squared that. I'm also aware that whether we are the current members of the Crown or not, and this lock get back in or not, it's gonna be an ongoing issue.

So putting the politics aside, knowing there's really little money what is lost by making it a nationally identified pest, uh, that would lifts the elevation of concern to then give you the potential tools, whether you are negotiating with a slot or, uh, like here or our lot.

So you get the tools and the funding to make this kind of, uh, an awareness campaign that we can all wrap around. Yeah, I'll get, I'll get John to expand on that, but I, I guess what I would say is, um, sure, you know, unwanted organism status, uh, you know, with all of these things, when, when they arise and when we find them, um, is absolutely worthy of considering and looking at whether they should be classified as those if they're not already.

Mm-hmm. However, what what I would say is, um, the unwanted organism status, um, is not necessary. All needed to actually coordinate action and get things happening and deal with these things. Uh, there's a lot that there's a lot that can be done, uh, without having that status. So it's, it's, it's not, it's not the deciding factor in terms of what we do, but I'll get John to, uh, expand and talk more to that specific point.

Sure. Thanks, Joe. Um, so we've, we, we've got a pest that's been here for a couple of decades, and at least in our region as well, establishing wealth widespread and causing impact and not, not a material impact at at that fact, at that point. Yeah, sure. Um, uh, the use of a UA status, um, uh, would be helpful if we wanted to utilize some of the ugly other regulatory tools that we've got.

Yes. In the by security act. So if we wanted to run, um, of a region, wanted to run a small scale management program, if we wanted to put a controlled area notice in place, we don't think those tools are the right tools for the, for the moment. Um, if, if we think they are, let's let, let's say we get a, we, we get a small spread to, uh, the Waikato, um, and it looks like it's manageable.

And the Waco Regional Council wanted to put a small scale management program in place. Um, we could, uh, flip the UI switch immediately. Um, the, the, the challenge we got with, with UI with this particular one, uh, relates to, um, uh, uh, the really strong similarity with gravel Brownsville, um, and the ability for people to identify them.

And we don't want to put farmers in particular in a position where they say mowing hay on their dairy unit, moving it down the road, um, to their runoff, and they have it, and they, and they have broken the law. So we think there's a better way to deal with it. And we agree with the committee entirely, that elevation to a national level and strongly, um, communicating the risks, what it looks like and what we can all do about it, is the thing is the probably the most critical thing to happen right now, but don't think UA status helps that,

and we just worry that Uua status might complicate it a little bit. Thank you, John. We don't need 'em to trigger those other tools at the moment. We Do need, okay. So it's a timing thing. So in the order that they were asked of me, Mike, Steve, Sue, Joe, and Den Miles and Rachel, uh, thanks, gee, I just, um, and I use the comment as the similarity to swap out is actually probably elevates the risk.

Mm. In, in, in one respect. Because, you know, if it's a, a nodding thistle, a nodding thistle, that's what it is. If it's something that's, you know, you risk misidentification, I think that's probably one of the big challenges, as you say, um, interested in the threshold for, again, um, and what would, um, what would trip the debt in one of those, because I sit there and think it's been identified in 2020, so I look at the, um, potential remediation or in the plan for, um, north and regional Council is not, uh, consulted next year, um,

will come into effect 2027. I mean, the clock's ticking, and we've got hay being made and transported and machinery going left, right center, all around the regions, all around New Zealand. And to my mind, I sit there and I go back to that 35 cent reduction in carcass weights, which would be catastrophic for our sector. And it just feels to me that there should be a lot more urgency.

Yeah. Yeah. So, so the threshold for, for a can. So, um, if we to put a can in place, um, there'd be, um, uh, movement controls, um, uh, and there'd be quite a high administrative burden because, you know, it's over quite a wide landscape. We, we think that we're better to go down there with education and information, um, and really push, you know, what we would call that sort of voluntary assisted end of compliance rather than the harder end of compliance for the moment.

The, the step down from a canned, Uh, our ACT's fairly blunt. Yeah, yeah. Um, yeah, not regulation, no. So step down from a can would be a small scale management program, which, which our regional council would run. Um, this has probably got beyond that. So the, the, the, uh, the access that you need to have degree of confidence you can deal with the problem in three years.

So no, probably not. And so then the next thing is the regional PE management plan. Um, and so that does give, uh, NRC some, some regulatory powers. And so they have managed to move it into their, um, RPMP, um, uh, not easy to do outside of the review cycle. They have managed to do that by displacing gravel groundswell, um, and they've applied the gravel groundswell, um, uh, good neighbor rule.

Um, my, uh, our expectation is that as they complete, complete the review, formalize it in their r uh, in their RPNP, they'll probably put some other regulatory controls around it. So regulation I think is not the, um, easiest thing for this particular, uh, weed in, in the state that we have it in.

Um, we think it's the information, um, uh, that what do I need to do about it? How do I identify it? Um, we think that's probably the place we need to go in order to best manage the pathways of spread. Thank you. Uh, Joe, you're up Live. Live and your camera's on mute. Thank you so much. Thank You, Steve. Thank you.

I just have a question about the, um, educational piece for farm for, you know, farmers and farming rural communities. You talk about it to me, I feel like it's being a bit reactive instead of proactive. And is there any plans to have, I guess, a nationwide educational program, uh, you know, like a, a bit of a, a road show around the country?

And I know that there'll be some places that are probably really likely to have an incursion of this, but would you not see the benefit or is, or a benefit to doing a proactive road show, um, where people can learn about what this particular part looks like in, in reality?

And also, you know, 'cause we hear about un maybe unverified, um, sightings on the East coast and things like that. So is it not best to be proactive rather than reactive? I'm just interested to know if there's any plans for that type of thing. Yeah, so having, um, observed the, uh, the other submissions in the rest of the hearing, we, but we, we agree, uh, that that proactive national communication needs to be set up.

It's not that there isn't anything being done at the moment. Uh, so, um, uh, NPIs, um, on farm, uh, advisory, uh, team, um, have been actively out there, uh, disseminating information, disseminating information about, um, uh, Madagascar Rag and others. But I think we just need some stronger coordination.

And so we, we, we are, uh, certainly intent on reaching into the current coordination group, so the council industry lead group, and finding a way to support them to, to lift that up. Yeah. Thank you Joe. Uh, Sue then, and then Rachel and Mar, my Question was actually answered before it was about why not, Right? All right, now, Rachel and Mars.

Yes. So I, Steve, sorry. Sorry, mate. Sorry. I guess, I guess I, I think the kind of the view from the submitters that we've heard from, and also I think from the committee, um, is that we just do need to see some more urgency around it because, um, you, you said that there were lots of other things that could be done, um, even if it didn't meet the threshold for some of the interventions from NPI, but I guess I'd like to know what are those things?

I mean, you've talked a little bit about them, but, um, that this does have potential to have a huge economic impact is already having an economic impact, but a greater economic impact if it were to spread. And so, you know, we've talked, I think of some of the previous hearings around things like, you know, the Diddy mow Clean Check dry campaign, because as a South island mp, I, I would hate for it to spread its way through the north island and get into the South island.

Um, and so rather than waiting till that happens, and then it gets lifted up to that status and everybody, you know, has to rush and do all the things and put millions and millions of dollars in to a project, why can't we put some more tools in now to actually stop it from spreading and support the region on the ground where it already is in existence?

Yeah. So look, I, I agree. Um, uh, you know, and, and we are, so, so what we are going to do though is, is lean in and help to make sure that some of these things drive and they get moving, uh, a bit quicker and get lifted up. Um, the national awareness is one bit of it on records. Um, you know, we've, we have heard, and we know that control control is really difficult.

Um, however, there could be some things that are worth exploring, but further biocontrol, et cetera. Um, so yeah, I think what we need to do quite quickly here is identify out of all of that, what are the ones that are the most feasible or likely to pursue and get agreement between us that these are the ones we really should lean into and look at.

Um, and then between us, uh, push those along and, and get them moving, um, with, with, with a focus and some urgency on them. Um, but yeah, look, um, we're, we're, we're absolutely, uh, you know, committed and up for that and for leaning in with, with the others, uh, to actually get, get this stuff moving and, and get some greater coordination across it.

So, supplementary Sue, Um, you talked about it being when it's un when it's not the WO status, how it would be, you saw it best managed by councils, and that was followed, that came oh, guards meeting the end of last year. But are you relying on, does that mean, I'm just trying to understand how the funding works. Does that mean you're relying on council money to do most of the, um, education program, or is the government NPI money as well or is it a combination, or do you attack it Well, a two pro?

Well, it's a combination, really.
